question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 224km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 12 units of medical kit (13kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (25kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 28%.
From 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 192km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 76 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 51 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 51min, 2nd: 66min, 3rd: 81min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 192km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (42kg each) and 3 units of construction material (64kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 100km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 78 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 49 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 49min, 2nd: 65min, 3rd: 81min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 215km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (65kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (54kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 176km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 76 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 43 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 43min, 2nd: 60min, 3rd: 77min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 232km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (41kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (5kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 195km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (57kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (50kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 165km waypoint, 7 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 86 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 62 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 62min, 2nd: 79min, 3rd: 96min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 193km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (68kg each) and 8 units of water barrel (20kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 23%.
From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 182km waypoint, 9 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 57 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 57min, 2nd: 72min, 3rd: 87min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 186km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (66kg each) and 8 units of medical kit (15kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 160km waypoint, 5 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 76 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 63 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 63min, 2nd: 79min, 3rd: 95min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 210km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (50kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (24kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 96km waypoint, 1 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 44 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 44min, 2nd: 58min, 3rd: 72min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 210km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (55kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (16kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 27km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 104km waypoint, 4 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 78 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 44 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 44min, 2nd: 61min, 3rd: 78min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 196km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (54kg each) and 7 units of construction material (65kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 23%.
From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 39km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 64 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 64min, 2nd: 79min, 3rd: 94min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 197km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 9 units of water barrel (20kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (8kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 132km waypoint, 5 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 80 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 42 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 42min, 2nd: 57min, 3rd: 72min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 217km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of water barrel (15kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (69kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.
From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 39km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 77 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 62min, 3rd: 76min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 184km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (50kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (15kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 60km waypoint, 4 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 55 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 55min, 2nd: 71min, 3rd: 87min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 206km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (51kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (61kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 28%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 77km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 79 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 49 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 49min, 2nd: 65min, 3rd: 81min, ...).

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 218km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of medical kit (11kg each) and 7 units of construction material (73kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 28%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 110km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 75 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 63 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 63min, 2nd: 80min, 3rd: 97min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 203km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (70kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (57kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 66km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 64 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 64min, 2nd: 80min, 3rd: 96min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (75kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (13kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 216km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 8 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 8 units of water barrel (19kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 65km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 35 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 35min, 2nd: 52min, 3rd: 69min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 190km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 10 units of relief supply box (60kg each) and 4 units of construction material (79kg each). The boat can travel at 41km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 40km waypoint, 1 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 75 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 55min, 3rd: 71min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 194km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (32kg each) and 6 units of construction material (78kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 168km waypoint, 9 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 81 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 62min, 3rd: 76min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 190km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (49kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (15kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 28%.
From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 160km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 83 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 65 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 65min, 2nd: 81min, 3rd: 97min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 207km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (65kg each) and 5 units of construction material (62kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 205km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (75kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (21kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 132km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 81 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 44 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 44min, 2nd: 58min, 3rd: 72min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 196km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (50kg each) and 6 units of construction material (69kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 96km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 49 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 49min, 2nd: 64min, 3rd: 79min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
solution:
STEP0=meta · STEP1=given · STEP2=worked solution · STEP3=answer and verification
[STEP 0] Problem meta

[STEP 1] Given (initial conditions & rules)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 209km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (77kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (68kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 23%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 150km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 79 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 35 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 35min, 2nd: 52min, 3rd: 69min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 216km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 13 units of construction material (64kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (31kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 104km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 46 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 46min, 2nd: 63min, 3rd: 80min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 205km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 12 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (67kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 27%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 77 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 37 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 37min, 2nd: 54min, 3rd: 71min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 215km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 14 units of water barrel (18kg each) and 3 units of construction material (62kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.
From 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 65km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 80 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 63min, 3rd: 79min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (69kg each) and 5 units of construction material (53kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 223km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (62kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (13kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 28%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 121km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 86 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 43 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 43min, 2nd: 58min, 3rd: 73min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 210km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (38kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (15kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 154km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 54min, 3rd: 69min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 199km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of relief supply box (50kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (21kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 165km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 190km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (56kg each) and 7 units of water barrel (16kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 28%.
From 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 70km waypoint, 4 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 77 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 44 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 44min, 2nd: 59min, 3rd: 74min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 210km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 15 units of construction material (71kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (34kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 132km waypoint, 6 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 77 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 66min, 3rd: 82min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 209km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of construction material (72kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (38kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 206km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (61kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (45kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 8:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 84km waypoint, 9 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 75 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 46 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 46min, 2nd: 61min, 3rd: 76min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 214km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (31kg each) and 7 units of water barrel (17kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 27km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 29%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 91km waypoint, 9 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 77 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 38 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 38min, 2nd: 52min, 3rd: 66min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 223km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of water barrel (23kg each) and 8 units of construction material (64kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 52km waypoint, 8 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 80 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 38 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 38min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 68min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 210km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (76kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (37kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.
From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 108km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 92 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 56min, 3rd: 71min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 207km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (64kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 81 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 46 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 46min, 2nd: 62min, 3rd: 78min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 229km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of construction material (55kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (41kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 30km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.
From 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 29%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 208km waypoint, 6 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 90 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 59 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 59min, 2nd: 73min, 3rd: 87min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 223km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of relief supply box (63kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (15kg each). The boat can travel at 25km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 104km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 81 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 43 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 43min, 2nd: 60min, 3rd: 77min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 192km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 12 units of relief supply box (57kg each) and 6 units of construction material (75kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 91km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 76 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 66min, 3rd: 82min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 200km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (53kg each) and 3 units of medical kit (11kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 91km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 78 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 55min, 3rd: 70min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 226km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of medical kit (14kg each) and 5 units of construction material (57kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 39km waypoint, 8 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 36 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 36min, 2nd: 51min, 3rd: 66min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 178km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (57kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 55km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 85 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 46 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 46min, 2nd: 62min, 3rd: 78min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 216km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of relief supply box (52kg each) and 7 units of water barrel (19kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 117km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 81 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 44 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 44min, 2nd: 60min, 3rd: 76min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
